title: Playful Tokyo & Kyoto with Littles
summary: Gentle-paced, stroller-friendly summer adventure focused on sensory play and short transit hops.

days.title: Arrival & Neighborhood Explorations
activities: Check into a family-friendly hotel near a major station (stroller access and coin laundry).
Walk through Shinjuku Gyoen for wide paths, shade, and space to run.
Early dinner at a family-friendly izakaya with private booths or a department store food hall.
days.proTip: Look for barrier-free station exits on signage—often marked with elevator icons—to avoid long staircases with a stroller.

days.title: Kid Museums & Gentle City Sights
activities: Ride the Yurikamome line for a driverless 'train ride' over the Rainbow Bridge—kids love sitting in the front row.
Visit teamLab Planets or a children’s museum with interactive exhibits (prebook tickets).
Cool down at indoor malls with kids’ play areas and easy food options.
days.proTip: Pack a small foldable picnic mat—many parents use them in parks, museums, or station waiting areas for quick snack breaks.

days.title: Kyoto Temples at Toddler Pace
activities: Take an early Shinkansen to Kyoto and head to Arashiyama before crowds build.
Walk the bamboo grove using a carrier instead of a stroller for narrower paths.
Visit a small temple with gardens where little ones can explore safely.
days.proTip: Buy ekiben (bento boxes) at the station so kids can eat whenever they get hungry on the Shinkansen, not just at set meal times.

days.title: Animal Encounters & Playgrounds
activities: Visit a small city zoo or aquarium with short walking loops.
Break up the day with a local playground stop so little ones can climb and slide.
Return early to your stay for bath time and a relaxed dinner nearby.
days.proTip: Check stroller and locker options at zoos and aquariums in advance so you can travel light inside.

days.title: Departure with Last-Minute Treats
activities: Let kids pick one small toy or snack from station shops as a farewell treat.
Use station play corners or quiet waiting areas to burn off energy before the train or flight.
Keep the final day schedule intentionally light in case of nap surprises.
days.proTip: Have one small surprise activity ready (like a new sticker book) for transit delays or airport waits.

title: Cozy Onsen Escape with Toddlers
summary: Warm indoor attractions and short walks, with kid-friendly hot springs.

days.title: Indoor Play & Warm Treats
activities: Visit an indoor kids’ playground or character-themed store inside a department store.
Warm up with taiyaki (fish-shaped cakes) and hot cocoa.
Early night to adjust to jet lag.
days.proTip: Always have a change of socks in winter—many indoor play areas and shrines require shoes off, and dry socks keep kids happy.

days.title: Snow Views & Onsen Ryokan
activities: Take a limited express train to a mountain onsen town with easy station access.
Check into a family-friendly ryokan with private onsen so parents can rotate bath time.
Enjoy a multi-course kaiseki dinner where staff can adapt dishes for kids.
days.proTip: Look for ryokan that offer kids’ yukata—toddlers love matching outfits and it makes for memorable photos.

days.title: Quiet Snowy Morning
activities: Take a short walk to see snow-covered streets and rivers from stroller-friendly paths.
Warm up with a simple café breakfast or bakery stop.
Build a mini snowman outside your ryokan if there is safe space.
days.proTip: Pack thin, layered clothing for toddlers so you can easily adjust between cold streets and overheated trains or shops.

days.title: Indoor Crafts & Story Time
activities: Plan a slow indoor day with coloring books, origami, or simple craft kits.
Rotate solo onsen time for each parent while the other reads stories with the kids.
Order an early, simple dinner to keep the evening calm.
days.proTip: Use hotel kettles to make instant miso soup or warm milk for a comforting toddler snack.

days.title: Return to Tokyo & Souvenir Stop
activities: Travel back to Tokyo with flexible train times to match naps.
Stop at a character street inside Tokyo Station for last-minute souvenirs.
Keep bedtime consistent so kids land at home with an easier transition.
days.proTip: Buy small, flat souvenirs (like stickers or washi tape) that pack easily even if your luggage is already full of winter clothes.

title: Anime, Arcades & Urban Adventures
summary: High-energy city days with late-night lights and interactive experiences.

days.title: Akihabara & Retro Arcades
activities: Explore anime shops, manga megastores, and capsule toy (gacha) halls.
Visit a multi-floor arcade and challenge each other at rhythm games.
Finish with a themed café or ramen spot popular with locals.
days.proTip: Set a gacha budget per teen to avoid overspending and make collecting more intentional.

days.title: Harajuku Style & Shibuya Crossing
activities: Walk Takeshita Street for crepes, vintage shops, and photo booths.
Visit Shibuya Sky at sunset for skyline views and Instagram-ready shots.
Cross Shibuya Scramble multiple times and film time-lapse videos.
days.proTip: Have teens download Suica/PASMO apps on their phones where available—tapping through gates independently gives them a sense of freedom while you still oversee routes.

days.title: Studio Tours & Anime Pilgrimage
activities: Visit anime-related landmarks or small museums featured in their favorite shows.
Plan a walking route that connects at least two anime or manga locations.
Finish with a character café or dessert spot.
days.proTip: Let teens pick one "must see" location each and co-design the route in a shared map app.

days.title: Tech District & Retro Gaming
activities: Explore a tech mall or electronics district for gadgets and accessories.
Find a retro arcade or game center to compare old and new games.
Grab dinner at a casual spot that stays open late.
days.proTip: Encourage teens to compare prices and features before buying electronics—it becomes a mini research exercise.

days.title: Day Trip to Yokohama or Kawagoe
activities: Take a short train ride to a nearby city for a change of scenery.
Walk historic streets or waterfront areas with plenty of snack breaks.
Return to Tokyo in time for an easy dinner near your stay.
days.proTip: Use day trips to practice teens navigating transfers and platform changes while you observe from a step behind.

days.title: Night Views & River Cruise
activities: Take an evening river cruise or bay cruise to see city lights from the water.
Pair the cruise with a simple noodle or curry dinner beforehand.
Stop for convenience store desserts on the way back.
days.proTip: Bring a lightweight layer for cooler breezes on the water, even in summer.

days.title: Free Exploration Day
activities: Give teens a budget and let them choose their own neighborhoods to explore in small groups.
Set clear meetup points and times at major stations.
Compare photos and favorite finds over dinner.
days.proTip: Use this day to simulate how they might travel with friends later, while still having your safety net in place.

days.title: Tokyo Disney or Theme Park
activities: Spend a full day at a theme park focused on coasters and shows.
Use mobile apps to track wait times and plan rides.
Stay for nighttime parades or light shows if energy allows.
days.proTip: Pick one "must do" ride per person first, then treat everything else as a bonus to keep expectations realistic.

days.title: Shopping & Packing Buffer
activities: Use this buffer day for last-minute shopping and arcade rematches.
Start packing and weighing luggage to avoid last-minute stress.
Choose an easy, familiar restaurant for a low-energy dinner.
days.proTip: Have each teen choose one item to leave behind or gift to make room for new finds.

days.title: Departure & Reflection
activities: Travel to the airport with extra time to handle souvenir tax refunds or check-in lines.
Ask each teen to share their top three moments and one thing they would change next time.
Capture a final group photo before boarding.
days.proTip: Use airport downtime to jot down lessons learned for your next trip—and for your interview story.

title: Powder Days & Night Cities
summary: Combine world-class snow with neon evenings in Tokyo.

days.title: Tokyo Night Views & Street Food
activities: Arrive in Tokyo and head to Asakusa for illuminated Senso-ji.
Ride up Tokyo Skytree or Tokyo Tower for night views.
Explore small izakaya streets, ordering shared plates for the table.
days.proTip: Encourage teens to try ordering in simple Japanese phrases—staff are usually patient and it becomes a fun challenge.

days.title: Ski Day Trip from Tokyo
activities: Take an early Shinkansen directly to a ski resort station.
Rent gear on-site and book half-day lessons if needed.
Soak in an onsen or foot bath at the end of the day before returning to Tokyo.
days.proTip: Reserve Shinkansen seats in advance for large families so everyone can sit together even on busy holiday weeks.

days.title: Gear Check & Local Neighborhoods
activities: Check winter gear, gloves, and layers before heading into longer snow days.
Explore a quieter neighborhood for lunch and small shops.
Turn in early to be rested for more ski time.
days.proTip: Dry gloves and socks overnight near gentle heat sources—not directly on heaters—to extend their life.

days.title: Second Ski or Snow Play Day
activities: Return to the same ski area or try a new mountain for variety.
Mix full runs with sledding or snow-play breaks for different energy levels.
End with hot drinks and simple comfort food.
days.proTip: Have teens track their own costs—lift tickets, rentals, snacks—to keep a realistic sense of winter sport budgets.

days.title: Onsen Recovery Day
activities: Visit an onsen or large public bath complex to rest tired muscles.
Try different bath types (outdoor, carbonated, jet baths) while following etiquette.
Grab dinner at a nearby izakaya or food court.
days.proTip: Remind teens to hydrate well on onsen days—the heat plus winter dry air can sneak up on them.

days.title: Historical Day Trip
activities: Take a day trip to a town with temples, shrines, or castles dusted in snow.
Climb a few staircases for viewpoints if paths are clear.
Try warm street snacks like sweet potatoes or mochi.
days.proTip: Use this day to balance action-heavy ski days with slower cultural learning for your trip narrative.

days.title: Shopping & Arcade Rematches
activities: Browse shops for winter fashion, souvenirs, or character goods.
Return to a favorite arcade to settle friendly rivalries.
End the night with hot pot or ramen.
days.proTip: Have teens choose experiences over objects when budgets get tight—like extra karaoke time instead of more souvenirs.

days.title: Photo Walk & Content Day
activities: Plan a route focused on capturing winter cityscapes, snow scenes, and neon.
Batch-edit photos or short videos back at the hotel.
Share a curated set with friends or family back home.
days.proTip: Ask teens to limit themselves to 10 favorite photos for the day to practice editing and storytelling.

days.title: Flexible Free Day
activities: Let everyone pick how to spend their own time with clear safety boundaries.
Use this day to handle any logistics or shopping missed earlier.
Meet for a final celebratory dinner.
days.proTip: This is a good day to test how independently teens can navigate before heading home.

days.title: Departure & Debrief
activities: Head to the airport with generous buffer time for winter transit delays.
Reflect on what worked best for ski days versus city days.
Capture your final "goodbye Tokyo" shot together.
days.proTip: Note how winter packing affected your experience so you can optimize layers and luggage next time.

title: Romantic Nights & Hidden Alleys
summary: Slow rhythms, evening walks, and intimate dining spots.

days.title: Lanterns & Riverside Strolls
activities: Check into a machiya-style stay in central Kyoto.
Walk through Gion’s lantern-lit alleys at dusk, keeping respectful distance from residents.
Dine along Pontocho Alley at a riverside restaurant with terrace seating.
days.proTip: Book restaurants that accept online reservations—small Kyoto spots often fill weeks in advance, especially on weekends and festival nights.

days.title: Sunrise Temples & Tea
activities: Visit Kiyomizu-dera right at opening for quiet temple grounds.
Stop at a traditional tea house for a matcha tasting.
Browse pottery shops and pick one shared keepsake for your home.
days.proTip: Travel with one shared digital photo album and add your favorite three photos per day—this keeps the story tight for your interview showcase.

days.title: Philosopher's Path & Café Hopping
activities: Stroll along the Philosopher's Path at an unhurried pace.
Pause at small neighborhood cafés for coffee and seasonal sweets.
End the day with a quiet dinner at a local bistro.
days.proTip: Choose one café to linger in without screens and use the time to reflect together on the trip so far.

days.title: Arashiyama Bamboo & River
activities: Visit the bamboo grove early to avoid mid-day crowds.
Take a riverside walk or short boat ride if weather allows.
Seek out a small soba or tofu restaurant for lunch.
days.proTip: Leave one hour unscheduled in Arashiyama to simply sit by the river or wander side streets.

days.title: Transit to Tokyo & Rooftop Drinks
activities: Ride the Shinkansen to Tokyo, using the time to read or journal.
Check into a hotel with city views.
End the night with rooftop drinks or a hotel bar with skyline vistas.
days.proTip: Reserve window seats on the Shinkansen for a chance at Mt. Fuji views on clear days.

days.title: Neighborhood Wanders & Hidden Bars
activities: Explore a bohemian neighborhood full of vintage shops and record stores.
Share small plates at a standing bar or tiny restaurant.
Finish with a cocktail bar tucked above street level.
days.proTip: Let one partner lead the evening "without a map" as a playful way to discover new spots together.

days.title: Art Museums & Garden Breaks
activities: Visit a museum or gallery that aligns with your shared interests.
Take a break in a nearby park or garden when your feet need a rest.
Have a light dinner so you can still enjoy a late-night dessert stop.
days.proTip: Choose one exhibit to go through slowly together instead of trying to see everything.

days.title: Day Trip for Two
activities: Visit a coastal or mountain town for a different pace.
Share snacks from local vendors as you walk.
Return to Tokyo for a simple dinner and an early night.
days.proTip: Use this day to test how you split navigation, language, and logistics as a team.

days.title: Free Day & Splurge Dinner
activities: Give yourselves a day with no fixed agenda to follow your energy.
Book one special-occasion dinner at a restaurant you've both been excited about.
Take a late-night walk through a favorite neighborhood afterward.
days.proTip: Treat this as your "anniversary" night even if the trip is not tied to a specific date.

days.title: Departure & Story Capture
activities: Travel to the airport with time to spare.
Capture a short voice memo or notes about what this trip meant to you as a couple.
Plan one simple ritual to bring home, like tea together on weekend mornings.
days.proTip: Frame your favorite trip photo together once you're home to keep the memory visible.

title: Snowy Onsen Retreat
summary: Quiet snow scenes, hot springs, and multi-course dinners.

days.title: Tokyo to Snow Country
activities: Take a scenic train to a ryokan town known for outdoor rotenburo (open-air baths).
Check into a room with a private open-air bath if possible.
Enjoy a kaiseki dinner in-room, moving at your own pace.
days.proTip: Ask the ryokan staff to time your dinner after your private bath session—you’ll appreciate the relaxed transition between experiences.

days.title: Snow Walks & Local Cafés
activities: Borrow yukata and clogs to stroll through the town’s narrow streets.
Visit a local café for hand-drip coffee and seasonal sweets.
End the day with a second onsen session under the night sky.
days.proTip: Pack a compact, quick-dry towel for onsen hopping—ryokan towels are often large and stay damp between baths.

days.title: Temple or Shrine in the Snow
activities: Visit a local shrine or temple blanketed in snow for peaceful photos.
Warm up with hot amazake or tea at a nearby shop.
Return to your ryokan for a quiet evening.
days.proTip: Carry micro-spikes or good-traction boots if you plan to walk on potentially icy paths.

days.title: Couples Spa & Massage Day
activities: Book a couples massage or spa treatment if available.
Alternate between reading, napping, and soaking in onsen.
Keep meals simple and light to match the slow pace.
days.proTip: Use airplane mode for most of the day and treat this as an intentional digital reset.

days.title: Transit Back Toward Tokyo
activities: Ride a mid-day train back toward Tokyo or another city base.
Check into a hotel with easy station access.
Take an evening stroll through a nearby neighborhood.
days.proTip: Pack snacks for the train so you can skip crowded station platforms at mealtimes.

days.title: Winter City Stroll
activities: Visit winter illuminations or seasonal pop-ups in central Tokyo.
Duck into cafés when you get cold and people-watch from the window.
Share a warming nabe or oden dinner.
days.proTip: Carry a compact umbrella—winter in Japan can bring both snow and rain.

days.title: Museum or Gallery Pairing
activities: Choose one museum and one small gallery or shop that inspires you.
Walk between them if sidewalks are clear and conditions are safe.
Discuss which pieces felt most resonant over hot drinks.
days.proTip: Use museum gift shops to pick up small pieces that remind you of the trip without taking too much suitcase space.

days.title: Day Trip by the Sea
activities: Head to the coast for winter ocean views and brisk walks.
Visit a seaside shrine or viewpoint.
Return to the city for a relaxed evening.
days.proTip: Bring a scarf and hat—the seaside wind can feel much colder than the temperature suggests.

days.title: Flexible City Day
activities: Use this open day to revisit any favorite spot or explore somewhere new.
Keep the schedule loose so you can follow weather and energy levels.
End with a simple dinner at a neighborhood spot near your hotel.
days.proTip: This is a good day to split up briefly if you each have a different "one more thing" you’d like to do.

days.title: Departure & Warm Memories
activities: Head to the airport with extra time for winter transit conditions.
Share your top three "winter magic" moments from the trip.
Make a quick list of what you would pack differently next time.
days.proTip: Carry one onsen-scented item or small bath salt home to recreate a piece of the trip later.

title: Street Food, Rooftops & Nightlife
summary: Shared plates, hidden bars, and flexible late starts.

days.title: Osaka Street Food Crawl
activities: Walk under Dotonbori’s neon signs and pick stalls for takoyaki and okonomiyaki.
Hop between standing bars (tachinomiya) for quick drinks and snacks.
Take group photos under the Glico running man sign.
days.proTip: Agree on a shared cash pool for small bites so no one is stuck splitting tiny individual checks all night.

days.title: Rooftop Beer Gardens
activities: Spend the day casually shopping or resting.
Head to a department-store rooftop beer garden for all-you-can-eat-and-drink plans.
End with late-night karaoke in a private room.
days.proTip: Use coin lockers near stations to store shopping bags before nightlife so you can move freely as a group.

days.title: Coffee & Alleyway Bars
activities: Sample specialty coffee shops across one neighborhood.
Take an afternoon nap back at your stay.
Explore narrow alleyway bars with only a few seats each.
days.proTip: Rotate who chooses the next bar or café to keep the day feeling co-created.

days.title: Day Trip to Kyoto or Nara
activities: Take a quick train ride to Kyoto or Nara for temples and shrines.
Share sweets like mochi or matcha soft serve between friends.
Return to Osaka for dinner in a quieter backstreet.
days.proTip: Pair one high-energy day (like Dotonbori) with a calmer temple day so the group doesn’t burn out.

days.title: Shopping & Game Night In
activities: Shop for clothes, cosmetics, or souvenirs as a group.
Pick up convenience store snacks and drinks.
Have a low-key game or movie night back at your stay.
days.proTip: Use this night to reset sleep schedules if the group’s rhythm has drifted.

days.title: Transit to Tokyo & First Night Out
activities: Ride the Shinkansen to Tokyo and check into a central neighborhood.
Grab an easy dinner near the station.
Explore one nightlife district, keeping plans open-ended.
days.proTip: Book seats together in advance so the whole group shares the same car and row.

days.title: Shibuya & Shinjuku Night
activities: Bounce between Shibuya Scramble, photo booths, and late-night ramen.
Walk through Omoide Yokocho or Golden Gai to peek into tiny bars.
End with dessert at a café that stays open late.
days.proTip: Set a regroup time and place in case the group naturally splits across venues.

days.title: Recovery Brunch & Vintage Shops
activities: Sleep in and then head to brunch in a relaxed neighborhood.
Browse vintage clothing, records, or bookshops.
Return early and keep the evening flexible.
days.proTip: Build in these "soft" days after big nights out to keep the trip sustainable.

days.title: Omakase or Izakaya Night
activities: Book a group-friendly izakaya or casual omakase spot.
Share dishes family-style so everyone tries something new.
Find a low-key bar or lounge to debrief the trip so far.
days.proTip: Check group dietary needs when booking, and choose places that can easily adapt.

days.title: Day Trip Adventure
activities: Spend the day exploring a different region together.
Take scenic photos and make inside jokes that will last beyond the trip.
Return to Tokyo ready for another low-key evening.
days.proTip: Use a shared album so everyone can contribute their best shots from the day.

days.title: Free Exploration Pods
activities: Split into smaller groups based on interests: shopping, cafés, arcades, or museums.
Set a single meetup restaurant for dinner.
Share highlights from each "pod" afterwards.
days.proTip: This is a good day for introverts to take some quieter solo time if needed.

days.title: Photo & Content Day
activities: Revisit favorite streets or viewpoints for better photos in good light.
Film short clips or TikToks together.
Back up all phones and cameras in the evening.
days.proTip: Nominate one friend as "content producer" to coordinate shots, while others just enjoy the moment.

days.title: Last Big Night Out
activities: Choose one last area to go big: karaoke, bars, or clubs.
Take group photos at the start and end of the night.
Walk home together or share cabs with a clear plan.
days.proTip: Agree on signals or code words if anyone needs a quieter corner or to call it a night early.

days.title: Packing & Souvenir Day
activities: Finalize souvenir shopping and snack stock-ups.
Pack methodically, weighing bags if needed.
Have an easy dinner close to your accommodation.
days.proTip: Buy shared snacks for the flight so no one is stuck with only airplane options.

days.title: Departure & Group Debrief
activities: Travel to the airport together with time for check-in and security.
Share one key memory you'll carry forward from the trip.
Plan your "next trip" idea while it’s still fresh.
days.proTip: Create a shared note capturing your favorite venues so future you—and future friends—can benefit.

title: Winter Lights & Local Izakaya
summary: Illuminations, hot pots, and cozy shared stays.

days.title: Tokyo Winter Illuminations
activities: Check out seasonal illuminations in Roppongi Hills or Shinjuku’s Southern Terrace.
Warm up with nabe (hot pot) at a local izakaya.
Stop by a convenience store for late-night snacks to share back at your stay.
days.proTip: Keep one shared group calendar with illumination and festival dates—many are short-lived but unforgettable in photos.

days.title: Day Trip & Onsen Finish
activities: Take a short train ride to a nearby town with historic streets and snack alleys.
Sample sweets and street food while exploring old warehouses or harbor views.
End at a spa complex with multiple baths and relaxation rooms before heading back.
days.proTip: Carry a lightweight scarf—it doubles as warmth on platforms, a modesty cover in shrines, and a pillow on trains.

days.title: Snowy Shrine Visit
activities: Visit a shrine or temple in light snow for atmospheric photos.
Warm up with hot drinks from a vending machine or café.
Finish with shared izakaya plates.
days.proTip: Remind everyone to bring gloves—touching snowy railings without them gets old quickly.

days.title: Indoor Arcade & Game Café
activities: Spend time in arcades and game cafés away from the cold.
Compete in rhythm games or co-op challenges.
Have a casual dinner nearby.
days.proTip: Set a time limit or budget for arcades so you still have energy for the rest of the night.

days.title: Sapporo or Northern City Hop
activities: Fly or take a long-distance train to a northern city known for winter festivals.
Check into accommodation walking distance from main sights.
Sample regional winter specialties for dinner.
days.proTip: Pack a smaller overnight bag for this segment so you’re not dragging full-size luggage through snow.

days.title: Snow Festival Day
activities: Spend the day exploring snow or ice sculptures and festival food stalls.
Take group photos at iconic installations.
Warm up in cafés or indoor spaces between walks.
days.proTip: Use heat packs in your pockets and boots to stay outside longer without getting uncomfortable.

days.title: Onsen Night with Friends
activities: Visit an onsen or sento as a group.
Share stories from the trip so far in the relaxation room.
End the night with convenience store snacks at your stay.
days.proTip: Review onsen etiquette together beforehand so everyone feels confident going in.

days.title: Transit Back South
activities: Take a train or flight back toward central Japan.
Use travel time to rest, edit photos, or play games together.
Keep the evening schedule light.
days.proTip: Build generous buffer time around winter travel days to absorb delays without stress.

days.title: Shopping & Indoor Markets
activities: Explore covered shopping streets or department store food halls.
Snack your way through different regional foods.
Return early and keep the night open.
days.proTip: Agree on a meeting point in busy indoor markets in case the group gets separated.

days.title: Illuminations Round Two
activities: Visit another set of winter lights in a new part of the city.
Try a different style of izakaya or hot pot restaurant.
Stop for late-night desserts or drinks.
days.proTip: Compare which illumination displays felt more magical and why—this helps sharpen your travel storytelling.

days.title: Free Day for Micro Groups
activities: Break into micro groups for highly tailored plans: shopping, photos, cafés, or rest.
Set a shared dinner spot as your anchor.
Keep the evening relaxed.
days.proTip: Encourage everyone to check in on their own energy level and choose accordingly.

days.title: Board Games & Cozy Night In
activities: Stock up on snacks and drinks from nearby shops.
Play board games, card games, or co-op video games together.
Use this as a mid-trip reset night.
days.proTip: Nights in are often the ones friends remember most—build at least one into every long trip.

days.title: Café Hopping & Viewpoints
activities: String together a route of favorite or new cafés across the city.
Visit an observation deck for daytime winter views.
Have a simple dinner to match the slower pace.
days.proTip: Limit each café stop to 45–60 minutes so you keep the day moving.

days.title: Souvenir & Packing Day
activities: Finish all souvenir shopping and snack stock-ups.
Pack bags carefully and weigh them if needed.
Choose a final izakaya or restaurant that suits the whole group.
days.proTip: Consolidate shared items—like snacks or gifts—to avoid duplicate purchases.

days.title: Departure & Highlight Reel
activities: Head to the airport together with extra time for winter conditions.
Share your top three personal highlights and one group highlight.
Start drafting a shared album or reel title on the way home.
days.proTip: Save your group highlight reel idea somewhere you’ll see it, so it actually gets made after the trip.
